caveats: The user may have run verification outside indexed agent sessions.

findings.suggestion: Use java.security.SecureRandom and produce a fixed-width, high-entropy token, e.g. `byte[] b = new byte[32]; new SecureRandom().nextBytes(b);` then hex/Base64URL-encode with fixed length. Remove the java.util.Random import.
findings.summary: resetToken() derives reset tokens from java.util.Random (source.java:5 `private static final Random RNG = new Random();`, source.java:11 `Long.toHexString(RNG.nextLong())`). java.util.Random is a 48-bit LCG whose internal state is recoverable from a single observed nextLong() output, so an attacker who sees one reset token can predict all subsequent tokens and take over accounts. This breaks the security guarantee of the password-reset flow (user data exposure / broken flow). Also, Long.toHexString drops leading zero nibbles, so token width varies and small values yield very short tokens.
findings.title: Password reset tokens generated with predictable java.util.Random (account takeover)

findings.suggestion: Actually apply the SecureRandom fix, remove/replace the stale BUG comment, add a test locking in the secure behavior, and run the build/test to produce fresh verification evidence before handoff.
findings.summary: Lines 7-9 contain a `BUG:` comment fully describing the insecure-PRNG flaw, but resetToken() (line 11) still exhibits it — the fix was described, not applied. This is a documented-but-not-fixed handoff: the comment reads as resolution while the code regresses. No unit test guards the generator, and the `rerun_relevant_verification` gate is unresolved (no build/test log attached).
findings.title: Documented bug shipped un-fixed; no test or verification (handoff gap)

findings.suggestion: Encode fixed-width bytes from SecureRandom (e.g. HexFormat.of().formatHex(buf) over a 32-byte buffer) so every token is the same length.
findings.summary: Even after switching generators, `Long.toHexString(...)` (line 11) omits leading zeros, so token strings vary from 1 to 16 hex chars. Variable-length tokens weaken effective entropy assumptions and complicate downstream parsing/validation.
findings.title: Long.toHexString yields variable-length tokens

nodes.label: rerun_relevant_verification

summary:
Risk-tiered review (lite) completed.

Single-file change adds a TokenGenerator whose resetToken() uses java.util.Random — a predictable LCG PRNG — for security-sensitive reset tokens. The in-code comment (lines 7-9) correctly identifies the flaw and prescribes SecureRandom, but the implementation (lines 5, 11) still ships the vulnerable version, a direct stated-intent-vs-code contradiction. This is a real, statically-provable high-severity security bug (CWE-338/CWE-330); no runtime artifact is needed to confirm it. The 'unpredictable token' invariant is asserted in prose but not enforced.

New TokenGenerator uses java.util.Random (a predictable LCG PRNG) to mint password-reset tokens. This is a critical, self-documented security/product-safety defect: a guessable reset token enables account takeover, breaking the reset flow's core guarantee. Confirmed statically — no runtime evidence needed because the insecurity of java.util.Random is an intrinsic property of the class. The only assumption that could soften this is that resetToken() is never used for real security-sensitive tokens, but the method name and comment contradict that.

A new TokenGenerator generates password-reset tokens from java.util.Random, a predictable LCG PRNG — a critical, exploitable weakness (seed recovery → token prediction → account takeover). The file even documents the flaw in a BUG comment but ships it unfixed, which from an agent-handoff standpoint is a documented-but-not-resolved deliverable with no test and no verification run. Fix by moving to SecureRandom with adequate entropy and fixed-width encoding, add a regression test, and rerun the build/test to satisfy the blocked verification gate.
